requirementCueLines: Students may earn a Biology BA with a General Biology Option. The Biology BS degree can be earned with one of 5 options: Ecology Evolution and Conservation Biology (EEC), General Biology, Molecular Cellular and Developmental Biology (MCD), Physiology, and Plant Biology. Minors in Paleo Biology and Marine Biology are also part of the curriculum offered to Undergraduates.
Admission Requirements
Minimum requirements for consideration
Minimum 2.0 grade in each of BIOL 180, BIOL 200, and BIOL 220; or in BIOL 240
Minimum 2.50 cumulative GPA for any supporting chemistry, physics, mathematics, and biology courses (or other courses that may apply to major requirements) completed at time of application.

Meeting minimum requirements does not guarantee admission. Other factors include overall academic record and difficulty of other courses completed; time to degree set by UW Satisfactory Progress Policy, including frequency of incompletes or withdrawal grades and number of repeated courses; relevant work and life experience; and record of honors.

All students must make satisfactory academic progress in the major. Failure to do so results in major-level academic notification, major-level warning, and major-level alerts, which can lead to dismissal from the major and being precluded from subsequently declaring the major in a future quarter. For the complete continuation policy, contact the departmental adviser or refer to the department website.

The BA Degree with an Option in General Biology is designed for students who desire a breadth of training throughout their program of study. Compared to the BS degree, the BA does not require physics or a third quarter of organic chemistry. This is an ideal program for students wishing to get the scope of the biological sciences without some of the supporting science sequences.
Completion Requirements
87-98 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (15-23 credits)
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238
Physics: not required
One of the following calculus/statistics sequences (9-10 credits)
MATH 124 and MATH 125
Q SCI 291 and Q SCI 292
Either BIOST 310, Q SCI 381 or STAT 311; Q SCI 482
Genetics: either GENOME 361, GENOME 371, or BIOL 340/FISH 340 (3-5 credits)
Natural history/biodiversity: one course selected from approved list (3 credits)
300- and 400-level courses, in addition to any 300-/400-level courses taken to fulfill requirements above. See department website for approved courses. (42 credits.)
Minimum 2.0 cumulative GPA for courses applied toward major requirements

The Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation option is for students interested in the origins, maintenance, or conservation of biological diversity. This option prepares students with strong system level approaches to problem solving, and careers in natural resources and conservation. A strong quantitative background is emphasized, and courses serve to develop skills in data collection, analysis, and communication. It is a degree option that allows students to explore courses offered through the College of Environment and Arts and Sciences.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
84 -104 credits
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (12-27 credits):
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, and CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143 or CHEM 145), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153 or CHEM 155), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 257, CHEM 258, CHEM 259
One of the following two-quarter sequences of mathematics (calculus/statistics) (9-10 credits):
MATH 124, MATH 125
MATH 134, MATH 135
Q SCI 291, Q SCI 292
Either BIOST 310, Q SCI 381, or STAT 311; Q SCI 482
Two quarters of physics (8-10 credits): one of the following:
PHYS 114, PHYS 115
PHYS 121, PHYS 122
PHYS 141, PHYS 142
Genetics (3-5 credits): either GENOME 361, GENOME 371, or FISH 340/BIOL 340.
Natural history/biodiversity (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Breadth (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Option Requirement (31 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.
*CHEM 162 is not required for this degree; however, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, and CHEM 239 are required by many professional programs and graduate schools, and that sequence does require CHEM 162.
Additional Degree Requirements:

Minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA for all UW courses applied toward major requirements, including required supporting courses (chemistry, physics, mathematics), introductory biology, and upper-division coursework. (A grade of 2.0 is not required in individual courses.)
Minimum 15 credits of 300- or 400-level BIOL courses taken through UW Seattle
Minimum 15 credits of 400-level BIOL courses

The General Biology option emphasizes breadth of training in Biology. As the most flexible among the options leading to a Science degree in Biology, students have input in the composition of their degree by choosing from the wide range of electives available through several different departments.
84-104 credits
Natural history/biodiversity (3 credits): one course selected from approved list.
Breadth (3 credits): one course selected from approved list. May also be used to meet the N atural history/biodiversity requirement #6 above.
Option Requirement (34 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.

The Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology option is for students seeking undergraduate training in the molecular, genetic, and cellular basis of life. These topics assist students in understanding the complex biological processes that underlie cellular function, disease processes, and embryonic development.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Inquiry requirement during the first two years.

The Physiology option emphasizes physiological processes from cellular to organismal levels. This is an attractive option for students interested in the internal processes of both animals and plants in relation to the systems and components that create a working living organism.

The Plant Biology option provides students with strong and broad training in plant biology, ranging from the cellular, physiological and systematic levels to an ecological, horticultural, and restoration perspective on a macro level.
82-102 credits
Option Requirement (29 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.

parsedDegreeMapBlockCandidates.title: Biology parsed official source requirements
sourceLineHints: Students may earn a Biology BA with a General Biology Option. The Biology BS degree can be earned with one of 5 options: Ecology Evolution and Conservation Biology (EEC), General Biology, Molecular Cellular and Developmental Biology (MCD), Physiology, and Plant Biology. Minors in Paleo Biology and Marine Biology are also part of the curriculum offered to Undergraduates.
Admission Requirements
Minimum requirements for consideration
Minimum 2.0 grade in each of BIOL 180, BIOL 200, and BIOL 220; or in BIOL 240
Minimum 2.50 cumulative GPA for any supporting chemistry, physics, mathematics, and biology courses (or other courses that may apply to major requirements) completed at time of application.

Meeting minimum requirements does not guarantee admission. Other factors include overall academic record and difficulty of other courses completed; time to degree set by UW Satisfactory Progress Policy, including frequency of incompletes or withdrawal grades and number of repeated courses; relevant work and life experience; and record of honors.

All students must make satisfactory academic progress in the major. Failure to do so results in major-level academic notification, major-level warning, and major-level alerts, which can lead to dismissal from the major and being precluded from subsequently declaring the major in a future quarter. For the complete continuation policy, contact the departmental adviser or refer to the department website.

The BA Degree with an Option in General Biology is designed for students who desire a breadth of training throughout their program of study. Compared to the BS degree, the BA does not require physics or a third quarter of organic chemistry. This is an ideal program for students wishing to get the scope of the biological sciences without some of the supporting science sequences.
Completion Requirements
87-98 credits

ownerTitle: Biology - B.S. Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation option
primarySourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation major requirements
sourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation major requirements
requirementCueLines: The Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation option is for students interested in the origins, maintenance, or conservation of biological diversity. This option prepares students with strong system level approaches to problem solving, and careers in natural resources and conservation. A strong quantitative background is emphasized, and courses serve to develop skills in data collection, analysis, and communication. It is a degree option that allows students to explore courses offered through the College of Environment and Arts and Sciences.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
Completion Requirements
84 -104 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (12-27 credits):
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, and CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143 or CHEM 145), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153 or CHEM 155), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 257, CHEM 258, CHEM 259
One of the following two-quarter sequences of mathematics (calculus/statistics) (9-10 credits):
MATH 124, MATH 125
MATH 134, MATH 135
Q SCI 291, Q SCI 292
Either BIOST 310, Q SCI 381, or STAT 311; Q SCI 482
Two quarters of physics (8-10 credits): one of the following:
PHYS 114, PHYS 115
PHYS 121, PHYS 122
PHYS 141, PHYS 142
Genetics (3-5 credits): either GENOME 361, GENOME 371, or FISH 340/BIOL 340.
Natural history/biodiversity (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Breadth (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Option Requirement (31 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.
*CHEM 162 is not required for this degree; however, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, and CHEM 239 are required by many professional programs and graduate schools, and that sequence does require CHEM 162.
Additional Degree Requirements:

Minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA for all UW courses applied toward major requirements, including required supporting courses (chemistry, physics, mathematics), introductory biology, and upper-division coursework. (A grade of 2.0 is not required in individual courses.)
Minimum 15 credits of 300- or 400-level BIOL courses taken through UW Seattle
Minimum 15 credits of 400-level BIOL courses

parsedDegreeMapBlockCandidates.title: Biology - B.S. Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation option parsed official source requirements
sourceLineHints: The Ecology, Evolution, and Conservation option is for students interested in the origins, maintenance, or conservation of biological diversity. This option prepares students with strong system level approaches to problem solving, and careers in natural resources and conservation. A strong quantitative background is emphasized, and courses serve to develop skills in data collection, analysis, and communication. It is a degree option that allows students to explore courses offered through the College of Environment and Arts and Sciences.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.

primarySourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: General major requirements
sourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: General major requirements
requirementCueLines: The General Biology option emphasizes breadth of training in Biology. As the most flexible among the options leading to a Science degree in Biology, students have input in the composition of their degree by choosing from the wide range of electives available through several different departments.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
Completion Requirements
84-104 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (12-27 credits):
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, and CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143 or CHEM 145), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153 or CHEM 155), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 257, CHEM 258, CHEM 259
One of the following two-quarter sequences of mathematics (calculus/statistics) (9-10 credits):
MATH 124, MATH 125
MATH 134, MATH 135
Q SCI 291, Q SCI 292
Either BIOST 310, Q SCI 381, or STAT 311; Q SCI 482
Two quarters of physics (8-10 credits): one of the following:
PHYS 114, PHYS 115
PHYS 121, PHYS 122
PHYS 141, PHYS 142
Genetics (3-5 credits): either GENOME 361, GENOME 371, or FISH 340/BIOL 340.
Natural history/biodiversity (3 credits): one course selected from approved list.
Breadth (3 credits): one course selected from approved list. May also be used to meet the N atural history/biodiversity requirement #6 above.
Option Requirement (34 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.
*CHEM 162 is not required for this degree; however, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, and CHEM 239 are required by many professional programs and graduate schools, and that sequence does require CHEM 162.
Additional Degree Requirements:

Minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA for all UW courses applied toward major requirements, including required supporting courses (chemistry, physics, mathematics), introductory biology, and upper-division coursework. (A grade of 2.0 is not required in individual courses.)

parsedDegreeMapBlockCandidates.title: Biology - B.S. General Biology option parsed official source requirements
sourceLineHints: The General Biology option emphasizes breadth of training in Biology. As the most flexible among the options leading to a Science degree in Biology, students have input in the composition of their degree by choosing from the wide range of electives available through several different departments.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
Completion Requirements
84-104 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)

CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239

primarySourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: Physiology major requirements
sourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: Physiology major requirements
requirementCueLines: The Physiology option emphasizes physiological processes from cellular to organismal levels. This is an attractive option for students interested in the internal processes of both animals and plants in relation to the systems and components that create a working living organism.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
Completion Requirements
84-104 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (12-27 credits):
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, and CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143 or CHEM 145), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153 or CHEM 155), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 257, CHEM 258, CHEM 259
One of the following two-quarter sequences of mathematics (calculus/statistics) (9-10 credits):
MATH 124, MATH 125
MATH 134, MATH 135
Q SCI 291, Q SCI 292
Either BIOST 310, Q SCI 381, or STAT 311; Q SCI 482
Two quarters of physics (8-10 credits): one of the following:
PHYS 114, PHYS 115
PHYS 121, PHYS 122
PHYS 141, PHYS 142
Genetics (3-5 credits): either GENOME 361, GENOME 371, or FISH 340/BIOL 340.
Natural history/biodiversity (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Breadth (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Option Requirement (31 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.
*CHEM 162 is not required for this degree; however, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, and CHEM 239 are required by many professional programs and graduate schools, and that sequence does require CHEM 162.
Additional Degree Requirements:

Minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA for all UW courses applied toward major requirements, including required supporting courses (chemistry, physics, mathematics), introductory biology, and upper-division coursework. (A grade of 2.0 is not required in individual courses.)
Minimum 15 credits of 300- or 400-level BIOL courses taken through UW Seattle
Minimum 15 credits of 400-level BIOL courses

parsedDegreeMapBlockCandidates.title: Biology - B.S. Physiology option parsed official source requirements
sourceLineHints: The Physiology option emphasizes physiological processes from cellular to organismal levels. This is an attractive option for students interested in the internal processes of both animals and plants in relation to the systems and components that create a working living organism.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.

primarySourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: Plant major requirements
sourceLabel: Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Biology: Plant major requirements
requirementCueLines: The Plant Biology option provides students with strong and broad training in plant biology, ranging from the cellular, physiological and systematic levels to an ecological, horticultural, and restoration perspective on a macro level.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
Completion Requirements
82-102 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (12-27 credits):
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, and CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143 or CHEM 145), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153 or CHEM 155), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 257, CHEM 258, CHEM 259
One of the following two-quarter sequences of mathematics (calculus/statistics) (9-10 credits):
MATH 124, MATH 125
MATH 134, MATH 135
Q SCI 291, Q SCI 292
Either BIOST 310, Q SCI 381, or STAT 311; Q SCI 482
Two quarters of physics (8-10 credits): one of the following:
PHYS 114, PHYS 115
PHYS 121, PHYS 122
PHYS 141, PHYS 142
Genetics (3-5 credits): either GENOME 361, GENOME 371, or FISH 340/BIOL 340.
Natural history/biodiversity (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Breadth (3 credits): one course selected from approved list
Option Requirement (29 credits): 300- and 400-level courses selected from list specific to this option. See department website for additional information.
*CHEM 162 is not required for this degree; however, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, and CHEM 239 are required by many professional programs and graduate schools, and that sequence does require CHEM 162.
Additional Degree Requirements:

Minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA for all UW courses applied toward major requirements, including required supporting courses (chemistry, physics, mathematics), introductory biology, and upper-division coursework. (A grade of 2.0 is not required in individual courses.)
Minimum 15 credits of 300- or 400-level BIOL courses taken through UW Seattle
Minimum 15 credits of 400-level BIOL courses

parsedDegreeMapBlockCandidates.title: Biology - B.S. Plant Biology option parsed official source requirements
sourceLineHints: The Plant Biology option provides students with strong and broad training in plant biology, ranging from the cellular, physiological and systematic levels to an ecological, horticultural, and restoration perspective on a macro level.

Suggested First- and Second-Year Courses: Students should concentrate on general chemistry and mathematics the first year, biology and organic chemistry the second year (see major requirements for specific courses). Transfer students: complete an entire sequence at one school if possible. It is not necessary, or even desirable, to complete the Areas of Knowledge requirement during the first two years.
Completion Requirements
82-102 credits
BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; or BIOL 240 (15 credits)
One of the following general and organic chemistry sequences (12-27 credits):
CHEM 120, CHEM 220, and CHEM 221
CHEM 142 (or CHEM 143 or CHEM 145), CHEM 152 (or CHEM 153 or CHEM 155), CHEM 223, CHEM 224
CHEM 142, CHEM 152, CHEM 162, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
CHEM 143, CHEM 153, CHEM 237, CHEM 238, CHEM 239
